claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility criteria to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I have the information I need to help you. To determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have one child, Alex, who's 17 and lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess Alex's eligibility, I need to ask:

Is Alex currently in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex actually left school recently, but they've just registered with the careers service to figure out what to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know. 

How long ago did Alex leave education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been fairly recent - just a few weeks ago.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just to confirm a few more details about your situation:

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you. And just to confirm - is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me now assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit based on what you've told me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news! **You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Even though Alex has left education, because they are 17 and have registered with the careers service, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks from when Alex left education. This is a special provision for 16 or 17 year olds who leave education or training and register with a government-sponsored careers service.

Since Alex left just a few weeks ago, you're well within this 20-week period. You should apply for Child Benefit to continue during this time.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about your Child Benefit eligibility?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and lives with the claimant in the UK. Although Alex has left education or training, they have registered with a government-sponsored careers service. According to the eligibility rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Since Alex left education just a few weeks ago and has registered with the careers service, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex for up to 20 weeks from when Alex left education. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and the claimant is responsible for Alex who lives with them.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) with their 17-year-old child Alex. Alex recently left education a few weeks ago but has registered with the careers service. Under UK Child Benefit rules, Child Benefit can continue for 20 weeks when a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service. Since all conditions are met and no one else is claiming for Alex, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Since Alex left education just a few weeks ago and has registered with the careers service, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex for up to 20 weeks from when Alex left education. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and the claimant is responsible for Alex who lives with them.

Under UK Child Benefit rules, Child Benefit can continue for 20 weeks when a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service. Since all conditions are met and no one else is claiming for Alex, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
